traps.name: Blackburne Shilling Gambit
traps.description: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Nd4!? Black offers a pawn to lure White into a tactical mistake. If White greedily plays 4.Nxe5?, Black replies 4...Qg5! threatening both Qxg2 (attacking Rh1) and Qxe5, winning material.
traps.refutation: White should decline with 4.Nxd4 exd4 5.O-O or 4.O-O, avoiding the trap entirely. After 4.Nxe5? Qg5! White is already in serious trouble.

traps.name: Légal's Trap
traps.description: A queen sacrifice trap: after 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 d6 3.Bc4 Bg4 4.Nc3 g6? 5.Nxe5! Bxd1?? 6.Bxf7+ Ke7 7.Nd5# checkmate. Black's greed in capturing the queen leads to a beautiful smothered-style mate.
traps.refutation: Black should avoid 5...Bxd1?? and instead play 5...dxe5 maintaining material equality. The key mistake is capturing the queen without seeing the mating net.

traps.name: Scholar's Mate Pattern in Italian
traps.description: A common beginner mistake where Black neglects f7 defence. After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 h6? 4.d4 exd4? White can play 5.Nxd4! threatening Bxf7+ followed by Qh5. Black must be vigilant about the f7 square.
traps.refutation: Black should develop normally with 3...Bc5 or 3...Nf6 instead of wasting a tempo with h6. After 3...h6? the bishop's pressure on f7 becomes dangerous.

traps.name: Noah's Ark Trap (Italian Variation)
traps.description: A positional trap where Black wins White's bishop on c4 using a pawn expansion. In certain Italian Game lines after an exchange on d4, Black plays ...a6 and ...b5, trapping the bishop on the a2–g8 diagonal with no safe retreat.
traps.refutation: White must avoid placing the bishop where it can be trapped. The best approach is to retreat Bd3 or Be2 before Black's b5 expansion gains tempo.
